![Prism → Sustainability Directory](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Prism-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)

# Policy Incentives for Regenerative Agriculture

![Geometric forms present a vision for the future of sustainable urban development featuring advanced eco technologies. Dark tones frame interconnected grey triangles with innovative tech driven architecture components to consider AI in smart building design, showcasing eco friendly material considerations. Bio inspired design is merged with cutting edge technology, emphasizing environmentally conscious practices for a future built upon sustainability integration, promoting ecological health through mindful urban ecosystems.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/futuristic-sustainable-architecture-with-bio-inspired-forms-tech-integration.jpg "Futuristic Sustainable Architecture with Bio Inspired Forms Tech Integration and AI Sustainability Development")

## Assess

The conversation surrounding [policy incentives Meaning → Policy Incentives represent the strategic deployment of economic and fiscal instruments by governing bodies to direct private sector and societal actions toward achieving predefined environmental and social sustainability objectives.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/policy-incentives/)  for [regenerative agriculture Meaning → A farming approach focused on restoring and enhancing ecosystems for environmental and social well-being.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/regenerative-agriculture/)  often begins with a sense of urgency, a recognition that our current food systems are exacting a heavy toll on the planet. Yet, to truly grasp the potential of these policies, we must move beyond the generalized anxieties of climate change and soil degradation. The core of the issue lies in the intricate dance between economic viability for farmers and the long-term ecological health of our agricultural landscapes.

For generations, [agricultural policy Meaning → Agricultural policy shapes farming practices and food systems to balance productivity, environmental health, and social equity for present and future generations.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/agricultural-policy/)  has been overwhelmingly geared towards maximizing yield, a singular focus that has externalized immense environmental costs. The challenge now is to design policies that internalize these costs and reward farmers for the positive externalities they can generate → clean water, enhanced biodiversity, and carbon sequestration.

At its heart, regenerative agriculture is a systems-thinking approach to farming that seeks to mimic natural processes. It encompasses a suite of practices—such as no-till farming, cover cropping, diverse crop rotations, and the integration of livestock—that work synergistically to rebuild soil organic matter, improve the water cycle, and increase biodiversity. The policy incentives designed to encourage these practices are equally diverse, ranging from direct [payments for ecosystem services Meaning → Payments for Ecosystem Services (PES) represents a market-based instrument and a policy mechanism that acknowledges the economic value of natural capital and the services it provides.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/payments-for-ecosystem-services/)  to crop insurance reforms and the development of robust carbon markets.

The effectiveness of these incentives, however, is not uniform. Their success is contingent on a complex interplay of regional context, market dynamics, and the socio-economic realities of farming communities.

![The scene showcases spheres and curved forms implying movement and interconnectedness against a gray backdrop reflecting a predicted sustainable future. Glossy white shapes juxtapose with sleek black elements promoting eco friendly balance in the eco system. A conceptual model demonstrating AI's role in sustainability for climate action, efficient technology reducing global carbon impact, and achieving equilibrium.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-spheres-exploring-the-predicted-future-concept-with-tech-in-ai-for.jpg "Sustainable Spheres Exploring the Predicted Future Concept with Tech in AI for a Balanced Outcome")

### The Current Landscape of Incentives

Globally, we are witnessing a nascent but significant shift in agricultural policy. In the United States, the Farm Bill has historically been the primary vehicle for agricultural support, often favoring commodity crops and conventional practices. However, recent iterations and supplementary legislation like the Inflation Reduction Act have begun to allocate more substantial funding towards conservation programs that support regenerative practices.

Programs like the Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP) and the Conservation Stewardship Program (CSP) provide financial and technical assistance to farmers willing to adopt more sustainable methods. Despite this progress, demand for these programs consistently outstrips available funding, indicating a significant appetite for change among producers that policy has yet to fully meet.

In the European Union, the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) is undergoing a similar evolution. The latest iteration of the CAP places a greater emphasis on environmental and [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  objectives, with “eco-schemes” designed to reward farmers for implementing practices beneficial to the environment. The EU’s “Farm to Fork” and Biodiversity strategies further underscore this commitment, setting ambitious targets for reducing pesticide use, increasing organic farming, and restoring biodiversity. A key component of this strategy is the development of a “carbon farming” initiative, which aims to create a new business model for farmers by rewarding them for sequestering carbon in their soils.

![Complex industrial machinery with its array of metal tubes and intricate mechanisms showcases advanced sustainability automated systems. A high angle and dark gray color scheme highlight the technology’s precision with sustainability focus, emphasizing automation's potential. It aligns resource management innovations, potentially contributing to an eco conscious, technologically enhanced automated process.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/automated-industrial-machinery-promoting-sustainable-future-engineering.jpg "Automated Industrial Machinery Promoting Sustainable Future Engineering Advancements in Resource Management Technology")

#### Data-Driven Policy and Market Mechanisms

A critical element in the design and implementation of effective incentives is the use of data. Data-driven agricultural policy allows for a more precise and targeted approach, moving away from broad-stroke subsidies towards interventions grounded in real-time insights and empirical evidence. This includes monitoring soil health, water quality, and carbon sequestration rates to verify the outcomes of regenerative practices. This data is not only crucial for policymakers but also for the development of private market mechanisms that can complement public incentives.

One of the most promising market-based approaches is the concept of Payments for [Ecosystem Services Meaning → Benefits humans freely gain from natural environment & ecosystems.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/ecosystem-services/)  (PES). PES programs create a framework where farmers are compensated for the environmental benefits they provide, such as clean water or enhanced biodiversity. These payments can come from a variety of sources, including governments, non-profits, and private companies seeking to offset their environmental impact. The development of robust and transparent carbon markets is another key area of focus, allowing farmers to generate revenue by selling carbon credits for the carbon they sequester in their soil.

The following table illustrates the primary types of policy incentives currently being explored and implemented:

| Incentive Type | Description | Example Programs |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Direct Payments** | Financial rewards for implementing specific regenerative practices or achieving certain environmental outcomes. | EU’s eco-schemes, Payments for Ecosystem Services (PES) programs. |
| **Technical Assistance** | Providing farmers with the knowledge and support needed to transition to regenerative systems. | USDA’s Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS). |
| **Crop Insurance Reform** | Adjusting insurance policies to recognize the risk-reducing benefits of healthy soil and regenerative practices. | Proposals within the U.S. Farm Bill. |
| **Carbon Markets** | Creating markets where farmers can sell carbon credits for sequestering carbon in their soil. | EU’s Carbon Farming Initiative, various private carbon credit programs. |

![A grayscale design conveys efficient strategies for resource allocation in a conceptual piece centered on creating a closed loop system of efficient design. It uses future technology, and demonstrates how advanced material reuse with the sustainability can be efficiently managed promoting circular economy models. Future sensors signify the use of modern environmental technology to analyze processes promoting optimized recycling with smart design for future resources, exemplifying the integration of renewable solutions, supporting expert-like sustainability approaches.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/conceptual-rendering-highlighting-circularity-in-future-sustainable-material.jpg "Conceptual Rendering Highlighting Circularity in Future Sustainable Material Technologies and Energy Resource Circular Economy Initiatives")

## Atrophy

The future of policy incentives for regenerative agriculture is not without its potential pitfalls. A future where these policies fail to achieve their intended outcomes is not only possible but is a scenario that can be precipitated by a number of interconnected failures. In this “atrophy” scenario, the initial momentum behind regenerative agriculture stalls, and the transition to more sustainable [food systems Meaning → The interconnected web of activities, people, and environments involved in food production, distribution, consumption, and disposal.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/food-systems/)  falters, leaving us with a degraded agricultural landscape and a more precarious food future.

One of the most significant risks is the potential for policies to be poorly designed or implemented. This could manifest as overly bureaucratic programs that are inaccessible to small and mid-sized farms, or as incentives that are insufficient to cover the costs and risks of transitioning to regenerative practices. If the financial support offered does not adequately compensate farmers for potential yield gaps during the transition period, or for the increased management complexity, adoption rates will remain low. This is a particular concern given that the current system of agricultural subsidies often favors large-scale, conventional operations, creating a powerful inertia that is difficult to overcome.

![An upward perspective shows symmetric units dripping with rainwater forming a central point, indicating urban technology infrastructure and AI enhanced security and surveillance in sustainable environmental monitoring programs. The image conveys themes of urban development and data driven decision making for better resource allocation and community safety using sustainable technologies and design contributing to smart sustainable cities, renewable resource usage, and a decreased environmental footprint. It hints at AI's growing importance in future city operations.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/symmetrical-abstraction-of-rain-soaked-surveillance-system-reflecting-ai-driven.jpg "Symmetrical Abstraction of Rain Soaked Surveillance System Reflecting AI Driven Sustainability in Future Urban Planning")

### The Perils of Greenwashing and Inadequate Verification

Another significant threat is the rise of “greenwashing,” where the term “regenerative” is co-opted by large corporations without a genuine commitment to the principles of ecological restoration. If policies are not coupled with robust monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems, it becomes difficult to distinguish between genuine [regenerative practices Meaning → Regenerative practices restore and enhance ecological and social systems for long-term health and vitality.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/regenerative-practices/)  and superficial claims. This could lead to a situation where public funds are directed towards operations that are not delivering real environmental benefits, eroding public trust and undermining the credibility of the entire regenerative agriculture movement.

The development of [carbon markets Meaning → Trading systems or taxes that assign a cost to greenhouse gas emissions to incentivize reductions.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/term/carbon-markets/)  presents a particularly acute risk in this regard. Without clear and scientifically rigorous standards for measuring and verifying carbon sequestration, these markets could become a breeding ground for low-quality or fraudulent credits. This would not only fail to deliver on the promise of climate mitigation but could also create a volatile and unreliable income stream for farmers, further discouraging adoption. The complexity and cost of accurate MRV can be a significant barrier, particularly for smaller farms, creating an uneven playing field that favors larger, more capitalized operations.

![A crystal prism encapsulates organic wheat representing preserved natural components for artificial intelligence sustainability integration. A textured vessel beside it disperses black beads across a glossy reflective base. This tableau invokes ethical sustainability principles.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-future-technologies-artificial-intelligence-and-preserved-organic.jpg "Sustainable Future Technologies Artificial Intelligence and Preserved Organic Matter Manifesting Environmental Ethics Concepts and Considerations")

#### Social and Economic Disparities

A future of atrophy is also one where the social and economic dimensions of the transition to regenerative agriculture are neglected. If policies are designed without a focus on equity, they could exacerbate existing inequalities within the food system. For example, if access to incentives and technical assistance is limited for historically underserved communities, including minority and indigenous farmers, then the transition to regenerative agriculture will be neither just nor equitable. Furthermore, a failure to invest in the necessary infrastructure, such as regional food hubs and processing facilities, could leave regenerative farmers without access to markets that will pay a premium for their products.

The potential for negative economic impacts on farming communities is a real concern. While regenerative agriculture has the potential to improve long-term profitability by reducing input costs and enhancing resilience, the short-term economic risks can be significant. Without adequate support, many farmers may be unable to weather the transition period, leading to farm consolidation and a loss of rural livelihoods. This underscores the importance of a holistic policy approach that addresses not only the environmental aspects of regenerative agriculture but also the social and economic [well-being Meaning → Well-being encompasses holistic health and happiness, supported by sustainable lifestyle choices that benefit both individuals and the environment.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/)  of farmers and their communities.

The following list outlines some of the key factors that could lead to a future of atrophy for regenerative agriculture policies:

![A low angle shot emphasizes the intricate metal support system of modern, sustainable architecture. Reflective glass panels and the geometric composition highlight innovative construction for resilient urban planning. Rings detail construction that promote green building materials, engineering and sustainability.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/futuristic-sustainable-architecture-metallic-structural-support-for-green.jpg "Futuristic Sustainable Architecture Metallic Structural Support for Green Building Materials Innovation")

## Ascend

In a more optimistic future, one we might call the “ascend” scenario, policy incentives for regenerative agriculture catalyze a profound and lasting transformation of our food systems. This is a future where the alignment of public policy, private investment, and consumer demand creates a virtuous cycle, driving the widespread adoption of regenerative practices and unlocking a cascade of environmental, social, and economic benefits. This scenario is not a utopian fantasy; it is a plausible future built on the foundations of intelligent policy design, technological innovation, and a collective commitment to a more sustainable way of life.

The cornerstone of this [ascend scenario Meaning → An Ascend Scenario fundamentally represents a future state where global efforts to achieve ambitious sustainability targets, such as net-zero emissions or circular economy principles, are not only met but demonstrably exceeded through proactive, systemic transformation.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/area/ascend-scenario/)  is the development of a coherent and holistic policy framework. This framework would move beyond a piecemeal approach to incentives, creating a synergistic system that supports farmers at every stage of their transition to regenerative agriculture. This would include not only direct payments for ecosystem services and robust carbon markets but also comprehensive support for research, education, and technical assistance. Crop insurance programs would be reformed to fully recognize the risk-mitigating benefits of healthy soil, creating a powerful financial incentive for farmers to adopt regenerative practices.

![The scene explores the potential of a sustainable future featuring coal material energy sources, innovative technologies, circular economy advancements, and environmental protection through conservation efforts. Spherical objects represent environmental remediation innovation with AI playing a critical role in resource allocation, alongside visions of renewable energy, technology development. Transparent surfaces reveal forward-thinking Prism scenario outcomes with AI assisted conservation driving our path towards sustainability.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-future-vision-exploring-ais-role-in-resource-management.jpg "Sustainable Future Vision Exploring AI’s Role in Resource Management Environmental Remediation Tech Development Scenarios and Possible Outcomes")

### The Power of Data and Technology

In this future, data and technology play a pivotal role in optimizing the effectiveness of policy incentives. Advanced remote sensing technologies, coupled with machine learning and artificial intelligence, would enable the accurate and cost-effective monitoring of environmental outcomes at scale. This would provide the foundation for a transparent and credible system of payments for ecosystem services, where farmers are rewarded for the specific, measurable benefits they provide. This data-driven approach would also enable the development of sophisticated decision-support tools for farmers, helping them to optimize their management practices for both profitability and ecological regeneration.

The ascend scenario also envisions a thriving bioeconomy built on the principles of regenerative agriculture. This would involve the development of new markets for a wide range of products and services derived from regenerative systems, from nutrient-dense foods to bio-based materials and renewable energy. This diversification of income streams would enhance the economic resilience of farming communities and create new opportunities for rural development. Public and private investment would flow into the development of the necessary infrastructure, including regional food hubs, processing facilities, and distribution networks, to support this burgeoning bioeconomy.

![A detailed study showcasing sustainable tech with dark cylindrical components set against blurred cream and gray objects reflects mindful design and environmental stewardship. This environmentally focused composition explores innovative eco friendly choices minimizing environmental impact promoting waste reduction strategies for a closed loop system and a future of improved sustainable manufacturing emphasizing regenerative economy for technological innovation for environmental sustainability. The object shows how a commitment to lowering the carbon footprint encourages ecological balance.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-tech-design-an-intricate-view-of-upcycled-materials-shaping-future.jpg "Sustainable Tech Design an Intricate View of Upcycled Materials Shaping Future Eco Friendly Products")

#### A Just and Equitable Transition

A crucial element of the ascend scenario is a deep commitment to social and economic justice. Policies would be designed with a specific focus on ensuring that the benefits of the transition to regenerative agriculture are shared equitably among all members of society. This would include targeted support for small and mid-sized farms, as well as for farmers from historically marginalized communities.

Land tenure reforms would provide greater security for farmers, enabling them to make the long-term investments required for regenerative agriculture. The result would be a more resilient and equitable food system, one that not only restores our ecosystems but also strengthens our communities.

The following table outlines the key pillars of a successful “ascend” scenario for regenerative agriculture policy:

| Pillar | Description | Key Enablers |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Holistic Policy Framework** | A coherent and synergistic set of policies that support farmers at every stage of the transition. | Integration of public and private incentives, long-term policy stability. |
| **Data-Driven Implementation** | The use of data and technology to optimize policy effectiveness and ensure accountability. | Advanced MRV systems, decision-support tools for farmers. |
| **Thriving Bioeconomy** | The development of new markets for products and services from regenerative systems. | Investment in infrastructure, research and development. |
| **Social and Economic Equity** | A commitment to a just and equitable transition for all farmers and communities. | Targeted support for underserved communities, land tenure reform. |

![Dark metal mechanisms interlock, illustrating an intricate system representing AI driven sustainability innovation and sustainability development goals. Fasteners and plates show engineering that favors durability and extended lifespan reducing waste. Sustainable solutions and resource efficiency define the intersection of technological advancement and future environmental impact reduction by fostering eco innovation and sustainable strategies.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-future-mechanism-abstract-depiction-of-interlocking-systems-for-ai.jpg "Sustainable Future Mechanism Abstract Depiction of Interlocking Systems for AI Driven Innovation and Sustainability Solutions")

## Aftermath

The journey towards a regenerative agricultural future is at a crossroads. The path we choose will be determined by the policy decisions we make today. The “assess” section has illuminated the current landscape of incentives, a promising but fragmented collection of programs and initiatives.

The “atrophy” and “ascend” scenarios have painted two divergent futures, one of stagnation and decline, the other of renewal and regeneration. The aftermath of our current efforts will be a direct reflection of our ability to navigate the complexities of this transition and to build a policy framework that is both ambitious and pragmatic.

![Detailed structure of intersecting recycled materials suggests an AI-enhanced, sustainable architectural prototype. Raindrops highlight eco-friendly design optimized for strength and resilience, illustrating smart cities solutions. The eco-conscious approach ensures minimal environmental impact showcasing a model for resilient future construction.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-future-concept-close-up-of-recycled-material-structure-showcasing.jpg "Sustainable Future Concept: Close-Up of Recycled Material Structure Showcasing AI Designed Framework for Resilient Infrastructure")

### Synthesizing the Scenarios

The “atrophy” scenario serves as a stark warning. It highlights the dangers of inaction, of half-measures, and of a failure to address the systemic barriers that stand in the way of change. A future where regenerative agriculture becomes a niche practice, accessible only to a privileged few, is a future where we have failed to meet the challenges of our time.

The “ascend” scenario, on the other hand, offers a vision of what is possible. It is a future where we have harnessed the [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  of policy to create a food system that is not only sustainable but also just and equitable.

The key to avoiding the pitfalls of the atrophy scenario and realizing the promise of the ascend scenario lies in a few critical areas:

![Dark gray pipes stacked illustrate sustainability in infrastructure providing water solutions a testament to innovative technologies reducing environmental effects. Durable water delivery systems like this represent forward progress incorporating eco friendly options by integrating long term clean infrastructure and emphasizing renewable construction methods and materials management. By designing systems focused on resource sustainability efforts are successful to mitigate water waste thus reducing the carbon foot print leading to cleaner renewable sustainability future development.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/durable-and-resilient-sustainability-infrastructure-innovative-pipe-system-for.jpg "Durable and Resilient Sustainability Infrastructure Innovative Pipe System for Eco Friendly Water Management Solutions")

#### Questions for the Future

As we move forward, we must continue to ask ourselves the hard questions. How can we design policies that are flexible and adaptable enough to meet the diverse needs of farmers in different regions and contexts? How can we best leverage the power of technology to support the transition to regenerative agriculture without creating new forms of inequality? And, perhaps most importantly, how can we cultivate a culture of collaboration and shared purpose that will be essential for navigating the challenges ahead?

The future of regenerative agriculture is not yet written. The choices we make in the coming years will determine whether we descend into a future of atrophy or ascend to a new era of ecological and social well-being. The potential is immense, but so are the stakes. The time for bold and decisive action is now.

![An intricately detailed macro composition reveals intersecting dark metal segments featuring rounded forms and securing bolts that represent a circular sustainability model. Raindrop elements cling to both the smoothed metallic segments and a porous rougher stony element. Designed for sustainable material practices these future component design models are developed with innovative technology and resource allocation AI managing the material cycle in a system created to model sustainable systems and water harvesting initiatives in a tech assisted sustainable future.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/contrasting-metal-and-stone-abstract-shapes-with-water-droplets-reflecting-a.jpg "Contrasting Metal and Stone Abstract Shapes with Water Droplets Reflecting a Circular Sustainable Future Concept Design")

## Introduction to Sustainable Agriculture Policies

Sustainable agriculture is a vital component of environmental conservation and ethical farming practices. As the global population continues to grow, the need for sustainable agricultural practices becomes increasingly pressing. Policy frameworks play a crucial role in promoting sustainable agriculture by providing a structured approach to implementing environmentally friendly farming practices. This article explores the various policy frameworks, regulatory measures, and institutional arrangements that support sustainable agriculture, and their implications for environmental ethics and conservation.

## Policy Frameworks for Sustainable Agriculture

Policy frameworks for sustainable agriculture are designed to promote environmentally friendly farming practices, improve crop yields, and ensure food security. These frameworks can be categorized into international agreements, national policies, and local regulations.

## Conclusion

Policy frameworks for sustainable agriculture are critical for promoting environmentally friendly farming practices and ensuring food security. By understanding the different types of policy frameworks, regulatory measures, and institutional arrangements that support sustainable agriculture, we can better appreciate the complex interactions between agriculture, environment, and society. As the global population continues to grow, it is essential that we prioritize sustainable agriculture practices and work towards a more environmentally conscious food system.

## FAQ

### What is sustainable agriculture?

Sustainable agriculture refers to farming practices that prioritize environmental stewardship, social responsibility, and economic viability.

### Why are policy frameworks important for sustainable agriculture?

Policy frameworks provide a structured approach to promoting sustainable agriculture practices, ensuring that farmers have the support and resources needed to adopt environmentally friendly practices.

### What are some examples of international agreements that promote sustainable agriculture?

Examples include the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations' initiatives, and the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD)'s efforts to reduce poverty and improve food security.

### How can local governments support sustainable agriculture?

Local governments can support sustainable agriculture by implementing policies that promote urban agriculture, community gardens, and agroecology practices.

### What is the role of certification programs in promoting sustainable agriculture?

Certification programs, such as the USDA Organic certification program, help to promote sustainable agriculture by providing a framework for environmentally friendly farming practices and ensuring compliance through regular inspections.

The publication draws upon the Global Policy Toolkit on Public Support to Organic Agriculture (IFOAM – Organics International, 2017) and other FAO publications (UNDFF Global Action Plan, FAO Legal Paper 108, FAO Legal Brief 8) and projects (PGS Vietnam developed with FiBL and CARES for TAP-AIS project, APIRAS & APAARI; CASIC in Cambodia). This policy brief incorporates some content from these publications when relevant.
The preparation of this guide benefited from the technical review of several FAO experts, such as Pierre Ferrand, Mauro Conti, and Guilherme Brady, whom we thank for their comments and contributions, which undoubtedly enriched the final document. Thanks are due to Cristina Grandi, Chief Food Security Campaigner at IFOAM – Organics International, as well as to all the technical referents who contributed at the preparation of this document. The editorial review by Hazel Haddon and the layout work by José Luis Castilla Civit are gratefully acknowledged.
1 Policy brief rationale The Global Action Plan (GAP) of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019– 2028 (UNDFF) supports governments in developing and improving context-specific legal and institutional frameworks to better tailor, prioritize and target interventions and investments for the support of family farming (FAO and the International Fund for Agricultural Development [IFAD], 2019).
IFOAM – Organics International advocates with local, regional and national authorities, as well as donor agencies and multinational organizations, urging them to accelerate their efforts to support and promote organic agriculture and agroecology as the most viable system for family farmers and smallholders (IFOAM – Organics International, 2011). With their approaches to soil, water and biodiversity conservation, as well as their integral and sustainable farm management, organic agriculture and agroecology can be highly productive, achieving family food security and improving incomes. Organic and agroecological farming systems are also more resilient than conventional systems that are highly dependent on expensive external inputs. Organic agriculture and agroecology have well-established practices that simultaneously mitigate climate change, build resilient farming systems, reduce poverty and improve food security (IFOAM – Organics International, 2011). Scope This document offers guidance to governments on how to support family farmers by promoting and implementing agroecology and organic agriculture approaches. It highlights a selection of relevant measures and provides real-world examples of successful implementation. The document will contribute to the implementation of the UNDFF Global Action Plan’s Pillars 1 and 7, as well as Pillars 4, 5 and 6, aiming at developing and reinforcing an inclusive policy environment for family farmers by facilitating full participation in the value chains of their choice (Committee on World Food Security [CFS], 2013).
Methodology IFOAM – Organics International has accumulated decades of experience in supporting countries in developing public policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology, providing assistance to numerous governments and collecting evidence of best practices all over the world. As a result of these efforts, the Global Policy Toolkit on Public Support to Organic Agriculture was published in 2017, intended as a living document to be regularly updated as IFOAM – Organics International keeps abreast of major developments and innovations in the area of supporting policies for organic agriculture and agroecology (IFOAM – Organics International, 2017). Policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology in the framework of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019–2028 2 Building upon the UNDFF’s Global Action Plan, international recommendations on public policies to support family farming, and in particular on FAO Legal Paper 108 (Vapnek and Boaz, 2021) and Legal Brief 8 (Blondeau and Korzenszky, 2022), this policy brief presents selected supporting policies from the Global Policy Toolkit on Public Support to Organic Agriculture, which addresses organic agriculture and agroecology and can be specifically conducive to family farming. The toolkit includes examples of implementation in different countries, regions and municipalities. Recent data on impact are presented as much as possible, depending on the availability of this information.
Legal Paper 108 uses 12 categories of legal and regulatory instruments mapped to the pillars of the UNDFF Global Action Plan to identify laws, programmes and policies that support family farming in diverse contexts. Legal Brief 8 builds on this paper, presenting different legislative and regulatory options to decision-makers to facilitate their appreciation of the complexity of the existing frameworks that underpin family farming. The legal brief emphasises four broad and interrelated areas that encompass prevalent issues for family farming: 1) access to resources and opportunities; 2) sustainability and traditions; 3) nutrition and healthy diets; and 4) resilience and socioeconomic status. In the brief below, relevant policies supporting organic agriculture and agroecology in relation to each of these four areas are discussed.
Key concepts Family farming: According to FAO’s working definition, family farming (including all family based agricultural activities) is a means of organizing agricultural, forestry, fisheries, pastoral and aquaculture production that is managed and operated by a family, and that is predominantly reliant on the family labour of both women and men. The family and the farm are linked, co-evolve and combine economic, environmental, social and cultural functions (FAO, 2013). Family farming is the main form of agriculture and is responsible for producing 80 percent of the world’s food. Recent research by FAO estimates that, worldwide, there are at least 550 million family farms (i.e. farms run by an individual or family and relying primarily on family labour); these operate 70–80 percent of farmland and produce roughly 80 percent of the world’s food in value terms. “The policies needed for the largest farms in the world are most certainly different from those needed for resource poor and land-scarce farms in the low and middle-income countries” (Lowder, Sánchez and Bertini, 2021).
Multifunctionality and sustainable food systems: Family farming is multifunctional, as it accounts for the majority of rural employment, most food production and the provision of ecosystem services, contributing to the preservation of natural resources and biological and cultural diversity in their rural settings. Smallholders and family farmers have therefore an essential role in food production and particularly in the implementation of sustainable food systems (IFOAM – Organics International, 2011). Strengthening the multidimensionality of family farming 3 is crucial to promoting social innovations that contribute to territorial development and food systems that safeguard biodiversity, the environment, and culture (see Pillar 7 of the Global Action Plan: FAO and IFAD, 2019).
Organic farmers are mostly smallholders, family farmers: Although representing only a small share of the total number of farmers worldwide, smallholders’ and family farmers’ contribution to sustainable food systems is particularly relevant in the certified organic sector, as more than 80 percent of the almost four million certified organic producers worldwide are smallholders in low- and middle income countries (Meinshausen et al., 2019).
Organic agriculture and agroecology for sustainable development: “Agroecology is the science of applying ecological concepts and principles to manage interactions between plants, animals, humans and the environment for food security and nutrition. All over the world farmers already apply this approach, which has a fundamental pillar in traditional and local knowledge” (FAO, n.d.). With its techniques in soil, water and biodiversity conservation, as well as its integral and sustainable farm management, organic agriculture is a well-defined subset of agroecology and can be highly productive, achieve family food security and improve incomes. Organic and agroecological farming systems are also more resilient than conventional systems that are highly dependent on external inputs, providing a way to achieve ecological, agronomic and socioeconomic intensification for family farmers. Adoption of organic agriculture or agroecology, though, is vitally linked to market access. It is imperative that family farmers, in particular those managing smaller areas, are not marginalized or unduly excluded from markets because of factors beyond their control. Agroecology is an integrated approach that simultaneously applies ecological and social concepts and principles to the design and management of food and agricultural systems (FAO, 2018), and it is therefore important to develop integrated policy packages in this area that address a wide range of cross-cutting challenges simultaneously (FAO, 2023).
Agroecology and organic agriculture can support engagement of women and youth: Agroecological and organic approaches have the potential to generate decent work opportunities for youth by developing inclusive, resilient and sustainable agriculture and food systems (High Level Panel of Experts [HLPE], 2019; CFS, 2022). These approaches can also increase gender equity and women’s empowerment by helping address the impacts of climate change on food systems and supporting the sustainable use, conserve or restoration of biodiversity, land, soils and the environment, while promoting healthy diets (CFS, 2023).
Territorial food systems: In order to generate significant action and measurable progress to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) by 2030, United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres has convened a Food Systems Summit (FSS) to raise global awareness and shape global commitments that can transform food systems to resolve hunger, reduce diet-related diseases and restore planetary health. The FSS secretariat have identified five action tracks: 1) ensuring access to safe and nutritious Policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology in the framework of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019–2028 food; 2) shifting to sustainable consumption patterns; 3) boosting nature-positive production; 4) advancing equitable livelihoods; and 5) building resilience. A territorial approach to sustainable food systems is essential to governance and planning of food systems and cuts across, can contribute to, and brings together the different action tracks (United Nations Capital Development Fund [UNCDF], 2021).
4 ©FAO/Giulio Napolitano 5 Policies supporting organic agriculture and agroecology, focusing on smallholder family farming It is essential that implemented policies recognize and support the role family farmers play in contributing to sustainable food systems. IFOAM – Organics International works on reducing barriers and establishing supportive mechanisms that allow organic agriculture and agroecology to contribute to food security, climate resilience and rural development. This includes boosting the recognition and uptake of organic and agroecological practices within the policies of governments at all levels, as well as facilitating support to family farmers as main stakeholders and agents of change in the transition towards sustainable territorial food systems. These policies are complex because of their multidimensional and holistic nature. For countries that seek to engage in this transition, the issue of selecting appropriate policies is crucial. These policies must be adapted to their own food and agricultural systems (Place et al., 2022). In any case, for their success a participatory approach is appropriate and necessary. Family farmers must be at the centre of the decision-making process, providing them with the tools to make informed decisions in a participatory manner. Governments may implement these through a variety of legislative and policy measures that include extension services and capacity building, public procurement, market creation, subsidies, and financial or tax incentives. This support may take the form of public policies presented in this brief. 1. Access to resources and opportunities Family farmers face constraints in accessing, controlling and owning resources and productive assets. A significant way for decision-makers to facilitate family farmers’ equitable access to resources and opportunities can be through legislation that supports the creation of organizations and cooperatives (Blondeau and Korzenszky, 2022).
1.1. Land tenure and other resources Access to land and other natural resources is fundamental for the viability and sustainability of family farmers’ livelihoods (IFAD, 2020). The SDGs recognize the fundamental role of small-scale food producers and family farmers, particularly of women and youth, and call for their access to land and other productive resources to reduce poverty and to improve their productivity and income. Policies should promote equitable tenure rights and access to land for all, including women and men, youth, and vulnerable and traditionally marginalized people (FAO, 2022). Policymakers should adopt support measures that promote youth and women’s access to land while engaging them in the production of healthy and sustainable food by adopting agroecological and organic practices. This is a way to address youth unemployment, gender inequality and other global problems, such as food insecurity and climate change. Policies and regulations related to land tenure rights have a strong impact on agricultural activity in general, be it conventional or organic agriculture. Restrictive approaches with policies that limit land acquisition and leasing favour the status instead of encouraging Policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology in the framework of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019–2028 6 transformation within the agriculture sector, thereby disproportionally affecting the development of organic farming, as often those interested in starting organic farming are young newcomers. At the same time, policymakers should not allow the unrestricted movement of capital into land acquisitions, as it can affect smallholders’ access to land. This trend may contribute to increasing the price of land, creating greater obstacles for newcomers, especially young people with agroecological business projects, who may lack capital.
Land policies can, however, mitigate the risks faced by (organic and agroecological) tenants by securing farmers rights and stimulating the adoption of sustainable agriculture practices, based on agroecology and the principles of organic agriculture. Land tenure policies that incentivize organic agriculture and agroecology, associated with extension, input and infrastructure support, benefit family farmers, particularly youth entering the agriculture sector.
Good practice: Supporting women in agroecology and organic production in Brazil In Brazil, the National Plan on Agroecology and Organic Production (PNAPO) 2013– 2015 established a series of objectives to promote the development of organic agriculture and agroecology in the country. One of these objectives (Objective 2) was to recognize and value the role of women in organic and agroecological production, strengthening their autonomy and economic development. As a result, this plan supported 556 women’s networks, benefiting 5 566 rural women. Furthermore, 960 professionals and political leaders were trained on financing women in organic and agroecological agriculture, which benefited 5 200 rural women in 20 different Brazilian states. Good practice: Supporting young farmers’ access to land in France In France, the Young Farmers’ Grant (Dotation jeune agriculteur) was introduced as a measure to facilitate access to land by providing young people with various forms of financial support. It is granted on condition that the applicant holds a professional agricultural capacity degree (i.e. that he or she has undergone specific training) and has designed a four-year business plan for an economically viable project that will generate sufficient agricultural income. This subsidy is 80 percent financed by the European Union’s Common Agricultural Policy (2018–2022 and 2023–2027) and 20 percent by the national budget of France (HLPE, 2021; Rural and Maritime Fishing Code, 1979). This aid can be increased by an additional boost if the projects include an agroecological or organic commitment, depending on the region of France (Territoires Bio, 2022). 7 1.2. Cooperatives and associations The promotion of cooperatives and associations (including farmers’ organizations and networks) is an important category of legislative and regulatory measures for countries seeking to strengthen the enabling environment for family farming, as well as for organic agriculture and agroecology. Historically, organic associations have initiated most elements of the organic sector, ranging from certification (with pioneers like the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland Soil Association and the United States of America California Certified Organic Farmers), to training and advice to farmers, organic consumer fairs, developing a national organic logo, and implementing consumer awareness campaigns. Particularly if they are federated at the national level, and provided that they are strong politically and financially, organic associations can play a decisive role in the development of the organic and agroecology sector, with specific benefits for smallholder family farming. They provide a platform for family farmers to cooperate to resolve production issues, purchase inputs or machinery that is used collectively and access new channels through joint marketing. Cooperatives and associations also provide relevant general agricultural services such as data collection, capacity building, political facilitation and ownership building, market development and advocacy for organic agriculture, agroecology and family farming. Governments can provide funds for organic associations to implement particular activities, (e.g. consumer education, capacity building of producers, or participation in policy design) as well as institutional support to organic associations by funding their core activities and expenses (e.g. staff salaries, contribution to administrative costs, or purchase of office equipment). Institutional support presents the advantage of empowering members of the organization to set priorities democratically for the organization.
“Governments wishing to encourage or strengthen cooperatives have a variety of tools at their disposal. First and foremost, legislation is needed to authorize the creation and operation of these types of groups. For example, the Bolivian Law No. 338 of 2013 on family farming addresses farmer associations in detail, as does Paraguay’s family farming Law No. 6286 of 2019. Legislative and policy measures must also support the financial stability of such associations as there can be, for instance, unexpected consequences of tax rules that can support or hollow out structures intended to benefit certain groups or types of organizations” (Vapnek and Boaz, 2021).
Policies and legislation may refer to specific provisions favourable to those organizations, such as increased space for participation in decision-making processes, tax exemptions, targeted and preferential credit lines, or access to extension and advisory services.
©FAO/Marco Longari Policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology in the framework of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019–2028 8 Good practice: Strengthening networks of agroecological and organic family farmers in Brazil In 2014, Brazil launched a programme named ECOFORTE to strengthen and increase the networks on agroecology, wild collection and organic agriculture, as part of the country’s National Plan for Agroecology and Organic Production (PLANAPO). The programme allocated EUR 70 million to support 30 networks of organizations promoting organic agriculture, agroecology and wild collection over two years.
The programme was implemented through public calls for proposals to select the networks to be supported, defined as groupings of at least three organizations, such as producers’ cooperatives or associations. The networks should mobilize, build capacity and disseminate information and technology to strengthen their organic and agroecological member organizations. These activities could include: purchasing of machinery and equipment; building or infrastructure development; support for value chain development and marketing; increasing women and youth participation; institutional capacity building through exchanges, workshops, training, meetings; research (feasibility and impact studies); financing and business plans; technical assistance for compliance with the national regulation on organic production; and integration with educational institutions or creation of study and research centres within these institutions. The financial assistance requested had to include 50 percent of infrastructure development costs and 50 percent of management, capacity building and technical assistance costs.
“Ecoforte was conceived as a Program for Strengthening and Expanding Agroecology, Wild collection and Organic Production Networks. The use of the network concept as a guiding reference for public action was one of the main novelties of this public policy instrument” (Schmitt et al., 2020).
The vast majority of networks that received support have family farmers and smallholders as their beneficiaries. “It has become possible to finance, by means of a single instrument (i.e. the same call for projects), investments considered as tangible (including machinery, equipment, vehicles, and facilities) and investments classified as intangible (technical assistance, educational and training activities, among others)” (Schmitt et al., 2020). “In addition, as initiatives supported by Ecoforte have shown, public policies oriented to the strengthening of territorial agroecology networks involving rural settlements and traditional communities are indispensable conditions for the reversal of trends that weaken family agriculture, contributing to the mobilization of segments of the rural population that have historically been marginalized by the State, as agents of economies of scope, redistributive and regenerative” (Schmitt et al., 2020).
After the success of the first edition, the second phase of ECOFORTE was launched in 2017.1 This edition is set to focus on strengthening networks and expanding dialogue and partnerships. 1. Currently, in a scenario marked by the launch of the PLANAPO 2024–2027, the resumption of the ECOFORTE programme is in process, and a new technical cooperation agreement has been signed by the national authorities, the Brazilian Agricultural Research Corporation, the National Bank for Economic and Social Development, the Banco do Brasil foundation, and Banco do Brasil.
Good practice: Promotion of organic and agroecological cooperatives in the European Union The European Union’s rural development policy is part of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), and it is often referred to as its second pillar. It complements the system of direct payments and supports the rural areas of the European Union, many of which face social and economic challenges. Through the Rural Development Program for 2014–2020, the European Union has been supporting cooperatives of organic farmers or related to agroecology. This included a special measure (Measure 16) related to “cooperation,” which encouraged farmers to cooperate and create innovative products. In addition to this, there were other measures to support the development of advisory services, research, and investment (European Commission, n.d.-a).
In December 2021, the agreement on the reform of the Common Agricultural Policy was officially adopted. With a new reform approved for 2023–2027, the Common Agriculture Policy needs to align with the European Green Deal by meeting the ambitions of the EU Farm to Fork and Biodiversity strategies. This will entail a greater emphasis on support measures for family farmers and other smallholders who embrace organic agriculture or agroecology and their cooperatives. Around 760 000, or 8 percent, of EU farms will receive support to participate in producer groups and organizations, short supply chain circuits and quality schemes (European Commission, 2022).
2. Sustainability and traditions Laws and policies can contribute to the sustainability of the family farm on two different levels: supporting the agrifood systems transformation through sustainable agricultural models, which promote biodiversity and the conservation of natural resources, as well as the use of environmentally sound practices; and promoting the multifaceted role of family farming through income diversification measures. 2.1. Participatory Guarantee Systems Participatory Guarantee Systems (PGS) are locally focused quality assurance systems. They certify producers based on active participation of stakeholders and are built on a foundation of trust, social networks and knowledge exchange. PGS represent an alternative to third-party certification, specially adapted to local markets and short supply chains. They are also sometimes referred to as “participatory certification.” They share a common objective with third-party certification systems in providing a credible guarantee for consumers seeking organic products. The difference is in the path to accomplish this, with the emphasis being on stakeholder participation and transparency. PGS offer numerous benefits, including improved access to organic markets through a guarantee system for small-scale producers (as these systems are much more affordable than third-party certification), increased education and awareness among ©FAO/HEBA KHAMIS 9 Policies to support organic agriculture and agroecology in the framework of the United Nations Decade of Family Farming 2019–2028 10 consumers (by involving them in the guarantee process), promotion of short supply chains and local market development, and farmer capacity building and empowerment. In other words, supporting PGS development is a way to promote agroecology and organic agriculture adoption, but also livelihood improvements through market access and empowerment of family farmers.
If a country regulates organic agriculture, it is crucial that the organic regulation does not hinder PGS development by deliberately or inadvertently not including them, thereby making these systems illegal. When PGS are recognized at the same level as third-party organic certification, PGS-certified producers can access the same policy benefits as third-party certified organic operators. But there are other ways in which governments can invest specific resources in promoting PGS development, such as by financing projects that set up PGS initiatives, or by providing financial support to existing PGS initiatives to cover expenses such as farmer training, committee meetings, development of standards and operating manuals, and communication and networking. They may also enable diverse marketing channels such as farmers’ markets through the provision of free sites, infrastructure, electricity and water.
Good practice: Support for PGS initiatives in Brazil In Brazil, the Ministry of Agrarian Development supports PGS initiatives and social control organizations (the other form of alternative verification systems allowed for direct marketing under the Brazilian organic regulations). For example, the ministry, in partnership with the Federal South Minas Institute, carried out a diagnosis of these organizations to identify existing initiatives and potential new ones. In 2016, around EUR 91 000 was allocated to support family farmers and technicians involved in these initiatives. The budget allocated for 2017 was even higher (around EUR 268 000) to implement the consolidation of the existing PGS initiatives, train hundreds of extension agents in PGS, support the establishment of new PGS initiatives and p

enriched_points.expanded_content: Establishing straightforward regulations helps farmers navigate compliance, ensuring consistent application of sustainable practices and enhancing the overall effectiveness of agricultural policies.
Strengthening institutions boosts resources and expertise available to farmers, facilitating access to knowledge and services essential for implementing green farming practices effectively.
Improving financial access enables farmers to invest in sustainable technologies and practices, ensuring they can transition to greener methods without financial strain.
Fostering partnerships between public entities and private sectors enhances innovation and resource sharing, driving advancements in sustainable agriculture and improving market access for farmers.
